prompt:
**Branch prediction.**

Consider the following code, executed on a machine with a Branch History Table (BHT).

**P1.A** The obtained result, in terms of mispredictions, is in line with theoretical characteristics of the two predictors? Effectively support your answer. (Describe a 1-BHT and a 2-BHT able to execute the code.)

**P1.B** List all the static branch predictors you know and discuss their performance in terms of mispredictions, if possible.

code.source:
li    $R1, 0
     li    $R2, 8000
FOR: LD    $F2, A($R1)
     LD    $F4, B($R1)
     LD    $F6, C($R1)
     FADD  $F4, $F2, $F4
     FADD  $F6, $F4, $F6
     SD    $F6, A($R1)
     ADDUI $R1, $R1, 8
     BNE   $R1, $R2, FOR

solution:
**Setup.** There is a single branch, `BNE $R1, $R2, FOR`. `$R1` starts at 0 and is incremented by 8 each iteration until it reaches `$R2 = 8000`, so the loop runs $(8000-0)/8 = 1000$ iterations. The branch is therefore **taken 999 times** (it loops back) and **not-taken once** (the final exit).

**P1.A ŌĆö 1-bit BHT vs 2-bit BHT.**
A *1-bit BHT* stores, per branch, the last outcome and predicts the same again. Starting from a cold/not-taken state, the first execution mispredicts (predicts not-taken, branch is taken), then the bit flips to taken and the next 998 taken iterations are predicted correctly. The single exit mispredicts (predicts taken, branch is not-taken). That is **2 mispredictions** over the 1000 iterations (one at entry, one at exit). If the entry state happened to be already taken, only the **1 exit** misprediction remains.
A *2-bit BHT* uses a saturating counter (strongly/weakly taken, weakly/strongly not-taken) and only changes its prediction after **two** consecutive opposite outcomes. Once the counter saturates to taken (after at most two iterations) it stays taken through the whole body; the single exit only **weakens** the counter from strongly-taken to weakly-taken without flipping the prediction, so the exit costs **1 misprediction** (plus the cold-start ones at entry).
This matches the theory: for a *single, long, non-nested* loop the two predictors are almost identical (Ōēł 1ŌĆō2 mispredictions out of 1000 ŌćÆ Ōēł 99.8ŌĆō99.9% accuracy). The well-known 2-bit advantage shows up only for **repeatedly entered / nested** loops, where the 1-bit predictor pays *two* mispredictions on every re-entry (last exit + first iteration) while the 2-bit predictor keeps its taken bias across re-entries. So here the result is in line with theory and the two are practically equivalent.

**P1.B ŌĆö Static branch predictors** (the prediction is fixed at compile/design time, with no runtime history):
- **Always not-taken (predict fall-through):** simplest, no target computation needed early. Mispredicts every *taken* branch; for this loop Ōēł 999 mispredictions (every backward branch). Poor for loop-dominated code.
- **Always taken (predict taken):** mispredicts every *not-taken* branch; for this loop only the single exit ŌćÆ Ōēł 1 misprediction. Good for loops but needs the target address available early, and bad for branches that are usually not-taken.
- **Backward-Taken / Forward-Not-Taken (BTFNT):** uses the sign of the branch displacement ŌĆö backward branches (loops) predicted taken, forward branches (if/else skip-forward) predicted not-taken. For this loop the backward `BNE` is predicted taken ŌćÆ only the single exit mispredicts (Ōēł 1). Captures common loop behaviour cheaply.
- **Profile-based / compiler-hinted (also delayed-branch / hint bits):** the compiler annotates the likely direction from profiling runs (or fills the delay slot). Accuracy depends on how representative the profile is; for a deterministic loop like this it predicts taken and reaches Ōēł 1 misprediction.

Static schemes cost almost no hardware but cannot adapt at runtime; their misprediction rate is bounded by the program's regularity. Dynamic predictors (1-bit/2-bit BHT, correlating) learn per-branch behaviour and do strictly better on data-dependent branches, while for a simple regular loop like this one even the static *always-taken*/BTFNT already reaches near-optimal Ōēł 1 misprediction.

prompt:
**MESI coherence ŌĆö analyse the pattern.** A three-processor system, each processor with a **direct-mapped, write-back** cache able to hold **one cache block**, and a memory of **three blocks** (B0, B1, B2). The full execution pattern is given below. For each cache the entry shows the **state** and, in parentheses, the **block** currently held; the memory columns show whether each block in memory is up to date (`yes`/`no`).

**P2.A** Is this a possible pattern with MESI (write-back caches, write-allocate, write-invalidate of other caches)? Write also a justification.

**P2.B** Compute the miss rate of the execution pattern (a miss = the required block is not already in cache). With hit time 10 ns and miss penalty 50 ns, compute the average access time for `Px`. What would you change in the memory subsystem to improve the average access time?

fillTable.caption: Given MESI execution pattern (read-only). Each Px/Py/Pz cell shows the cache state and, in parentheses, the block held; Mem columns show whether the memory copy of each block is up to date.

solution:
**P2.A ŌĆö Walk the transitions.** Recall the MESI invariants for write-back, write-allocate, write-invalidate caches: a *read miss* brings the block into the requester (Shared if another cache also has it, Exclusive if it is the only copy); reading a block that another cache holds **Modified** forces that owner to write back and downgrade to **Shared** (memory becomes up to date); a *write* must **invalidate every other cache's copy** and leaves the writer in **Modified** with memory **not** up to date; at most one cache may be in M/E for a given block at any instant.

Checking the rows against these rules, the given pattern is **inconsistent with MESI** ŌĆö several steps cannot occur:
- **t1 (`Px read block 2`):** the requested block is 2, but `Px` ends as **Exclusive (0)** ŌĆö a read must load the *requested* block into the reader, so `Px` should hold block 2 (`Shared`, since `Py`/`Pz` also have it), not block 0. A read that does not bring the requested block into the reader is impossible.
- **t2 (`Pz write block 2`):** the result shows `Py = Exclusive (2)` **and** `Pz = Modified (2)` at the same time. A write must invalidate all other copies, so `Py` cannot stay valid (it should be `Invalid`); two caches cannot simultaneously hold block 2 with one in M and one in E.
- **t3 (`Px read block 2`):** `Px` reads block 2 while `Pz` is `Modified (2)`; the table leaves `Pz` still **Modified (2)** and memory B2 still **no**. Reading a Modified block must trigger a write-back by `Pz` and downgrade `Pz` to **Shared** (memory B2 ŌåÆ `yes`). Here that did not happen.

Any one of these violations is sufficient: **the pattern is not producible by a correct MESI protocol.** (Later rows such as t18 `Px read block 1` staying `Modified` are themselves fine ŌĆö that is a read hit on a block `Px` already owns in M ŌĆö but the early rows already break coherence.)

**P2.B ŌĆö Miss rate and AMAT for `Px`.** A miss = the required block is not already resident in that cache (each cache holds a single block). Counting `Px`'s own accesses:
- t1 `Px read B2`: miss (cold) 
- t3 `Px read B2`: `Px` held B2 from t1 ŌåÆ **but** after t2 invalidation issues aside, it must re-fetch ŌåÆ miss 
- t6 `Px read B2`: `Px` held B2 (S) but was invalidated at t5 (`Invalid (2)`) ŌåÆ miss 
- t9 `Px write B0`: `Px` held B2, not B0 ŌåÆ miss 
- t13 `Px read B1`: `Px` held B0 (M) ŌåÆ miss 
- t17 `Px write B1`: `Px` held B1 (S) from t13 ŌåÆ **hit** 
- t18 `Px read B1`: `Px` held B1 (M) ŌåÆ **hit**

So `Px` makes **7 accesses with 5 misses ŌćÆ miss rate = 5/7 Ōēł 0.71 (Ōēł 71%)**. (Overall, across all three processors the pattern has 19 accesses and most are misses because each cache holds only one block ŌĆö a single-block direct-mapped cache evicts the previous block on essentially every new reference.)

Average access time for `Px`: $AMAT = hit\_time + miss\_rate \times miss\_penalty = 10 + (5/7)\times 50 \approx 10 + 35.7 = 45.7\text{ ns}$.

**Improvement.** The dominant cost is the tiny **single-block, direct-mapped** cache: every reference to a different block is a conflict/capacity miss because the one resident block is evicted. Increasing the number of cache blocks (a larger cache), adding **set associativity**, or inserting a small **victim cache** lets each processor keep more blocks resident, cutting the miss rate and pulling `Px`'s AMAT back toward the 10 ns hit time.

prompt:
**Tomasulo with ROB.** The following RISC-V code was executed on a Tomasulo+ROB machine; the execution table is given below. **Assumptions:** I8 branch is predicted not taken, thus it executes I9 sequentially. The misprediction is recognized at commit time, after which all wrongly speculated instructions are flushed. Integer operations have a separate CDB than Mem/FP operations.

**P3.A** Consider true data dependencies only. List all of them. Are they honored in the table above? (Mark "+" for each that turns into a hazard.)

**P3.B** Consider name dependencies only. What are they? Are they honored, and how? (Mark "*" for each that becomes a hazard.)

**P3.C** Consider control dependencies and structural hazards. What are they? Are they honored, and how? (Mark "ŌĆó".) Then: Is there a Tomasulo configuration that could respect the shown execution? If yes, describe ALL architectural requirements; if no, motivate.

**P3.D** Calculate the CPI of this architecture (ARCH1) for the given execution table.

**P3.E** ARCH1 runs at 700 MHz, ARCH2 at 500 MHz. Which CPI must ARCH2 have to be faster than ARCH1?

code.source:
li   t0, 0
L1:     li   t6, 2
        mul  t3, t0, t6
        li   t1, 0
L2:     add  t4, t0, t1
        addi t1, t1, 1
        li   t2, 100
        blt  t1, t2, L2
        mul  t5, t0, t0
        addi t0, t0, 1
        li   t2, 1000
        blt  t0, t2, L1

fillTable.caption: Given execution table (read-only). The mispredicted tail I9ŌĆōI12 is flushed and never commits.

solution:
Number the instructions I1..I12 in program order (I1 `li t0,0` ŌĆ” I12 `blt t0,t2,L1`).

**P3.A ŌĆö True (RAW) data dependencies.** A consumer reads a register a producer wrote:
- `t0`: I1ŌåÆI3, I1ŌåÆI5, I1ŌåÆI9, I1ŌåÆI10 (I9/I10 are on the flushed path)
- `t6`: I2ŌåÆI3
- `t1`: I4ŌåÆI5, I4ŌåÆI6, I6ŌåÆI8
- `t2`: I7ŌåÆI8, I11ŌåÆI12 (flushed path)
- `t0`: I10ŌåÆI12 (flushed path)

They *are* honored ŌĆö every consumer starts execution after its producer has produced the value (broadcast on the CDB). The ones that actually force a **stall/hazard** (the consumer's START EXE is delayed waiting for the producer's result) are marked **+**:
- I3 `mul` needs `t6` from I2 (WB cycle 6) and `t0` from I1 (WB cycle 5); it cannot START until those are on the CDB ŌćÆ START 7. **(+)**
- I8 `blt` needs `t1` from I6; I6 writes back at cycle 10, and I8 also waits behind the ALU/CDB ŌćÆ START 12. **(+)**
- I5 `add` needs `t1` from I4 (WB 8) ŌćÆ START 9. **(+)**

The `li` immediates have no source register, so their START is bound only by issue/structural timing, not by a true dependence.

**P3.B ŌĆö Name dependencies.** Same register reused for unrelated values:
- **WAW:** `t2` written by I7 and I11; `t1` written by I4 and I6; `t0` written by I1 and I10; `t2` again I11 (path).
- **WAR:** I8 reads `t2` (from I7) before I11 overwrites `t2`; I8/I5/I6 read `t1` before later redefinitions.

These are **honored by register renaming**: each redefinition allocates a fresh ROB entry / reservation-station tag, so readers capture the correct version and out-of-order writes never corrupt an earlier reader. Because renaming removes them, they do **not** cause execution stalls ŌĆö so under a correct Tomasulo+ROB **none of them turns into a real hazard** (no **\*** needed); they are listed only as eliminated name dependences.

**P3.C ŌĆö Control dependencies and structural hazards.**
- *Control:* I8 (`blt ŌĆ”,L2`) and I12 (`blt ŌĆ”,L1`) are the control points. I9ŌĆōI12 are control-dependent on I8. I8 is predicted **not-taken** but is actually **taken** (it loops back to L2), so the sequentially fetched I9ŌĆōI12 are on the **wrong path**; they execute speculatively and are correctly **flushed** when I8 commits (cycle 19). **(ŌĆó)**
- *Structural:* there is a single multiplier ŌĆö I3 `mul` (7ŌåÆ12) is long-latency, and I9 `mul` must wait for the multiplier (I9 START 10, COMPLETE 15) ŌćÆ a structural contention on the MUL unit. **(ŌĆó)** The CDB is also a shared resource, but the assumption of a *separate integer CDB from Mem/FP* avoids a write-back conflict when an integer result and a Mem/FP result complete in the same cycle.

*Is there a Tomasulo configuration respecting this execution?* **Yes.** Requirements: in-order **issue** (1 instr/cycle), out-of-order **execution** through reservation stations with register renaming via a **ROB** (Ōēź 12 entries to hold all issued instructions including the speculated tail), in-order **commit** that detects the I8 misprediction and flushes I9ŌĆōI12; functional units with the observed latencies ŌĆö an ALU (1-cycle effective for `li`/`add`/`addi`/`blt`) and **one** pipelined/long-latency **multiplier** (Ōēł 5ŌĆō6 cycle latency: I3 7ŌåÆ12); **two CDBs** (one integer, one Mem/FP) so coincident write-backs do not conflict; and speculation support (branch prediction + recovery at commit).

**P3.D ŌĆö CPI of ARCH1.** The execution starts at cycle 1 and the **last committed** instruction (I8) commits at **cycle 19**. The instructions that actually retire are the 8 correct-path ones I1ŌĆōI8 (I9ŌĆōI12 are flushed). Counting useful committed work: $CPI = \dfrac{\text{cycles}}{\text{committed instructions}} = \dfrac{19}{8} \approx 2.4$. (If one instead counts all 12 *issued* instructions, the raw CPI = 19/12 Ōēł 1.58; the difference is exactly the ~4 wasted speculation slots. We take the useful-work figure **CPI Ōēł 2.4**.)

**P3.E ŌĆö Break-even CPI for ARCH2.** Execution time for a fixed instruction count $N$ is $T = CPI \times N / f$. ARCH2 is faster Ōćö $T_2 < T_1$ Ōćö $\dfrac{CPI_2}{f_2} < \dfrac{CPI_1}{f_1}$ Ōćö $CPI_2 < CPI_1 \cdot \dfrac{f_2}{f_1} = CPI_1 \cdot \dfrac{500}{700} = CPI_1 \cdot \dfrac{5}{7}$. With $CPI_1 \approx 2.4$: $CPI_2 < 2.4 \cdot 5/7 \approx 1.7$. (Using the raw 1.58 instead gives $CPI_2 < 1.13$.) In general, ARCH2 must reach a CPI below $5/7 \approx 0.714$ of ARCH1's CPI to be faster.
